data.title: Does Bond Market Instability Result In The Fed Easing Sooner Than This Summer?
data.description:
Rick Rieder, BlackRock’s CIO, held his monthly call with investors on Monday, April 7th at 7 am central time. Usually, this call is held the first Thursday of the month, but with the market action last week, Rick pushed it off until Monday, to give it a few more days.

This table was page 8 of BlackRock’s slide packet, and if readers click on the image and enlarge it, while the two slides at the top of the page show that the US is the world’s largest importer in the world (not a surprise, probably due in no small part to the US being the largest economy in the world with annual GDP growth of around $30 trillion per year), the slide in the upper-right hand corner shows the US economy is much less reliant on trade for growth. Of the 9 countries listed, the US is the least of the countries or trade blocs, like the EU, that is reliant on imports for growth.

China is just above the US in the “trade reliance for growth” slide in the upper-right hand corner.

The fact is, this surprised me, and not a little.

The current administration is now engaged in a game of trade chicken versus China, and with the leverage still in America’s favor (given the above chart), and ultimately, you would think the US should prevail. The one challenge is that Xi Jinping, can simply tell the Chinese people, “look, you’re going to have to endure this however long it takes”. Xi doesn’t have to run for re-election.

Way back in 1998, around the time of the Russian debt default, John Meriweather, formerly of Salomon Brothers, founded Long-Term Capital Management, ran into trouble as the Street started taking opposite positions in his hedge fund’s Russian credits, and it ultimately caused Alan Greenspan to ask the major commercial and investment banks at the time to bail out Long -Term Capital. (This was the first real whiff of “systemic risk” that would ultimately rear its ugly head in 2007 and 2008.)

The point is, at least according to the book, “When Genius Failed” by Roger Lowenstein, and numerous articles that followed the collapse, Greenspan was getting quite concerned about the corporate bond spreads, and their relationship to Treasuries (and this was about more than just spread-widening I believe), and Greenspan chose to lower the fed funds rate in September 1998, while the actual market – meaning the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq – didn’t bottom until October 1998.

Greenspan ultimately reduced the fed funds rate from 5.25% in September ’98 to 4.50% into ’99, and it lit the stock market like a Roman candle. (This was in the period from 1995 to 1999 when the S&P 500’s “average, annual return” was 28.5% per year.)

So, what’s the point?

The point of relating 1998 Long-Term Capital memory is that what is happening within treasuries, might motivate the Fed and FOMC and Powell, to move sooner rather than later, also supported by the very favorable drop in the March, Core CPI and overall CPI, and the PPI data this week.

The “inflation expectations” component of this morning’s U of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Report was not as favorable as the March CPI data, and that’s got everybody’s attention. The year-ahead inflation expectations metric within the U of M report shot higher to 6.7% from 5%.

The “basis trade” might have a liquidity component to it, and the FOMC will not tolerate too much of that.

High-yield credit spreads jumped to an average of +437 (per the Bespoke data) as of this morning, from last week’s +350 over the equivalent treasury. That’s the highest spread seen since the +415 on 11/3/2023.

The spread-widening may not solely be the expectation of a weaker economy, but could also be a function of the treasury volatility.

Again, the point being the FOMC and Jay Powell may be easing sooner than was originally expected.

Summary/conclusion: Take this for what it’s worth. Some of the “basis trade” comments, and the leverage therein with the basis trade, resulted in me thinking about Long-Term Capital and that period in 1998.

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq peaked in late July ’98 and then investors watched the market really trade down hard in August ’98 and then the first half of September ’98, and while I work alone, and don’t have fellow portfolio managers or traders to yap with, there was an element in the financial media and the research at during late July, early August ’98 of “what’s driving this ?” until David Faber broke the story about Long-Term Capital before Greenspan cut the fed funds rate. (I believe Faber broke the story before the first fed funds rate cut, but that could be wrong.)

If you think the markets were volatile around tariffs, the Nasdaq corrected 35% from its late July ’98 high to its early October ’98 low) before rocketing higher in the Q4 ’98.

Illiquidity isn’t good for the bond markets. Rick Santelli, the CNBC economics reporter that reports from the floor of Chicago’s CME, talked a little about the “basis trade” and illiquidity this week in one of his reports and he didn’t yet think it was critical.

The Fed, and some of the Fed governors, like Austan Goolsbee, have said that the Fed’s monetary policy remains well above the neutral rate. The good March inflation data this week is a plus. Inflation expectations were not.

Once again, Monday morning and any news over the weekend, will drive another set of opinions.

None of this constitutes a recommendation or advice. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investing can or does involve the loss of principal even over short periods of time. None of the information above may be updated, and if updated, may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NVR: Expect Some Weak Quarters, But The Investment Thesis Remains Strong
data.description:
The Investment Thesis

NVR, Inc. (NYSE:NVR) is a highly profitable, low-risk home builder that has proven it can successfully navigate the market's cyclicality.

The company keeps executing its capital allocation strategy by decreasing the share count quickly, and the $2.5B available on its balance sheet could be deployed at any time.

Run by an experienced management that owns a significant stake in the company, NVR should keep benefiting from the accumulated housing shortage.

Company Overview

NVR is one of the largest single-family home builders in the U.S. with a market cap of $22B, over $10B in revenues, and 22,000 new homes delivered per year.

The company operates on the East Coast and has a particular business model where it acquires finished building lots under purchase agreements.

Unlike most home builders, NVR does not engage in land development. Instead, it deposits 10% of the purchase price and sells its homes in advance.

The strategy is a result of its bankruptcy in 1992. Since then, the company shifted its business model to reduce risks and generate higher returns on invested capital. As shown in the chart below, the strategy worked well.

Growth Drivers

The key factors that explain NVR's impressive growth are:

  • Increase in volumes and market share
  • Price increases at inflation rates
  • Margins expansion
  • Opportunistic acquisitions
  • Buybacks

Over the last three decades, the company has increased the number of homes delivered per year by 5.4% CAGR, which is way above the population and new home construction growth rates.

Although NVR has not significantly expanded into other regions of the country, its market share on the East Coast has notably increased.

While NVR does not have pricing power, it acts as a hedge against inflation and has been able to increase prices at inflation rates (3.3% CAGR for 30 years).

On top of the 8.7% growth from prices and volumes, its business model benefits from operating leverage.

While gross margins have remained stable over the years, its SG&A costs and interest expenses have grown at much lower rates compared to revenues, resulting in higher net income margins.

In addition, NVR has executed a top-notch capital allocation strategy by aggressively reducing the share count.

Although its capital allocation strategy is focused on buybacks, NVR has also closed some acquisitions over the years.

In 1986, NVR acquired Ryan Homes for $312M, a considerable acquisition at the time.

A decade later, it acquired Fox Ridge Homes for $12.5M and improved its mortgage division in 1999 by acquiring First Republic Mortgage Corporation for $3.7M.

The latest acquisitions were Marc Homebuilders in 2005 for $7.5M and Heartland Homes in 2012 for $14.3M, NVR's premium homes segment.

Low Profile Management

As one would expect from NVR's results, the company is run by an outstanding management team.

NVR's management owns 7.8% of the company, a significant percentage for a $22B company, showing strong alignment with shareholders.

One of the facts I view positively is that annual reports haven't changed for 30 years. They are clear, straightforward, and have all the relevant information shareholders need to know. They do not include fancy charts.

The company also does not provide guidance, hold earnings calls, or release expensive presentations, instead, they focus on what truly matters, the business.

This investor relations policy reminds me of other great companies such as Constellation Software (TSX:CSU:CA) or Old Dominion Freight Line (NASDAQ:ODFL).

Why do I view this as highly positive? Because I don't have the feeling the company is trying to sell me something.

The management knows that higher valuation multiples do not benefit them, as they will reduce the share count at a slower pace.

Short-Term Slowdown

I view NVR as a company worth owning for the long term, but I expect weakness during the upcoming quarters.

After a 10% increase in volumes during 2024, the backlog has come down significantly in Q4 2024, anticipating lower revenues in the next few quarters.

As shown in the chart below, revenues follow the backlog with a delay of around two quarters.

Considering the recent decline in new orders, I expect a decrease in revenues during Q1 2025.

Although the second half of the year tends to be affected by seasonality, the recent decline in new orders (-15.2% Q/Q) and backlog (-12.2% Q/Q) is above average.

In addition, the new order cancellation rate has increased to 16.9%, up from 13% a year ago.

The volume decline has been mainly driven by a slowdown in the South East region, which is also facing price adjustments.

In the Middle Atlantic area, NVR's biggest market, the decrease in volumes has been modest and prices remain strong.

Based on the latest report released by the National Association of Home Builders, new building permits in the Mid-Atlantic for single-family homes increased by 5% Y/Y in February.

The expected softness in the market is not exclusive to NVR, but the overall industry. After significant completion during 2024, demand for new construction is decreasing after the post-pandemic spike.

While I expect lower volumes in the following quarters, higher prices might mitigate the impact on top-line results.

For now, prices are not showing any sign of weakness. As long as mortgage rates remain high, there won't be an oversupply in the secondary market.

Despite my short-term forecast, the investment thesis remains solid from a long-term perspective.

Supported By The Housing Shortage

One of the key factors for my NVR investment thesis is the accumulated housing shortage in the U.S., which is causing, among other factors, a rapid increase in prices.

After a significant oversupply in the years preceding the 2008 financial crisis, the market took some years to absorb it.

However, new construction has not met the demand in the following years, and the U.S. housing market has accumulated a significant shortage of around 3.5M homes.

To meet the new demand, home builders should deliver 1.3M homes per year, but to cover the current shortage, new constructions should average 1.7M homes per year until 2030.

In recent years, even with the increase in supply, home builders have been delivering around 1.5M homes per year.

Considering that the market is not supplying the number of homes needed to cover the shortage, NVR has ample room to grow in the future.

Fair Value

Unless there is a higher-than-expected decline in revenues, NVR's stock is undervalued at current prices.

In recent years, the industry has enjoyed an increase in home prices above inflation due to the demand/supply unbalance, driving higher operating margins.

In my base scenario, I take a conservative approach, assuming margins return to their long-term average (15%) and corporate tax rates are not reduced by the Trump administration.

With a 3.5% decrease in share count per year, a 3.5% growth rate after 2030, and a 17x LTM net income multiple (10-year average), the stock presents a 25% upside potential from current prices.

I use a 9% discount rate based on a 1.08 beta, a 3% cost of debt, and a 9.4% cost of equity.

The revenues forecasted in the base case scenario are way below the analyst consensus, as shown in the image below.

Pessimistic Scenario

Let's imagine the economy sharply declines as a result of uncertainty around tariffs.

Higher costs for raw materials could lead to further margin contraction. Despite inflationary pressures, home prices may decline due to weak demand.

In the pessimistic scenario, to which I assign a 20% probability, using a 10% discount rate based on higher cost of debt and lower valuation multiples, the stock is overvalued by 15%.

Optimistic Scenario

In the most optimistic scenario, the Trump administration cuts corporate tax rates to 15%, and after a couple of weak quarters, NVR returns to its long-term trend.

After 2030, the company will grow in line with inflation plus the replacement of existing homes.

In that scenario, to which I assign a 10% probability, the stock presents an 85% upside potential.

Risks

The main risks for the investment thesis are, in my view, the lower geographic diversification compared to other home builders, a sharp decline in home prices, and higher building material costs.

Unlike D.R. Horton (NYSE:DHI) or Lennar Corporation (NYSE:LEN), which operate nationwide, NVR is concentrated on the East Coast.

Until now, home prices have been a tailwind for NVR's top-line figures, but if there is a sharp decline in demand, the company could suffer from both lower volumes and prices.

The recently implemented tariffs could lead to an increase in the prices of raw materials.

While the company does not disclose the percentage sourced from abroad, the U.S. imports around 30% of the softwood lumber consumed in the country from Canada. The second-biggest supplier is China, with $2.1B of wood products exported to the U.S. during 2024.

The situation is changing rapidly and news comes out daily, so it is difficult to predict the outcome, but the possibility of higher import prices is likely to occur.

Takeaway

Taking into account the estimated fair value in each of the presented scenarios and the assigned probabilities, NVR is trading below its fair value of $8,700 per share.

The downside in a negative scenario is limited compared to the upside potential in the base and optimistic scenarios. Therefore, I assign NVR a buy rating.

Although I expect some weak quarters as a result of the backlog decline, the long-term investment thesis remains solid.

The company has a highly profitable business model, a net cash position of $1.5B, and is run by an experienced management team with significant skin in the game.

Despite the short-term headwinds, NVR should keep benefiting from the shortage in the housing market.

data.title: Church & Dwight: A Solid Operator, Trades At A Price
data.description:
In the fall of 2023, I believed that Church & Dwight Co., Inc. (NYSE:CHD) found itself in a transition year. Momentum was solid on the topline, but margin pressure was observed during inflationary times.

With CHD shares commanding a premium, I was upbeat on the reduced leverage (which provided financial room for M&A activity) yet the premium awarded to the shares deterred me from getting upbeat on the shares. Forwarding in time, the business has seen a decent 2024, and issued a solid guidance for 2025 (although the question can be asked if this remains in place, of course).

This, in combination with reduced leverage is quite upbeat, however, current premium valuations in the high-twenty times earnings multiples, make it hard for me to get upbeat with a near to medium term perspective.

Premium Brand Collection

Church & Dwight is a $6 billion consumer brand business which is comprised out of 7 so-called power brands: TheraBreath, Hero, Vitafusion, Arm & Hammer, Waterpik, Batiste and OxiClean. Some 70% of sales are generated from these 7 power brands, complemented by the revenue contribution of smaller brands.

About 80% of sales are generated at home, complemented by smaller international activities. Other than a tiny specialty production business, the company's sales are split evenly between household and personal care revenues. Some two-thirds of the sales are tied to premium products and the remainder by value, as value is likely into fashion with consumers in these inflationary days.

Over the past two decades, the company has seen sales quadruple from $1.5 billion in 2004, to over $6 billion in 2024, as the company constantly seeks acquisition targets. Such targets typically include number 1 or 2 share brands, high-growth and high-margin business, asset light business models, and potential to leverage centralized functions.

With the business posting solid organic growth rates of an average of 4% per annum over the past decade, in recent years the company has seen volume struggles. This was notably the case in the inflationary 2022, although that volumes recovered slightly in 2023 and 2024.

All this means that the business is a pretty solid long-term performer, although the performance has been a bit choppy in recent years, as solid growth has been accompanied by stable operating margins around 20% of sales. The company has delivered on tiny net buybacks as well, with about five percent of the shares bought back over the past decade.

All this has resulted in solid returns for investors as a $1 stock in 1995 traded at $8 per share in 2005, traded at $40 in 2015, as shares first broke the $100 mark in 2022. Ever since, shares have largely traded in a $70-$110 trading range, with shares now trading around the $100 mark.

About The Valuation

Early in 2024, the company reported its 2023 results with revenues up 9% to $5.87 billion on which sound GAAP operating profits of $1.06 billion were reported, with GAAP earnings reported at $3.05 per share, based on a share tally of 247 million shares. Revenue growth was split pretty evenly between organic growth (at just over 5%) which in itself was largely driven by pricing, with volumes up nearly a percent. This was complemented by the contribution from M&A efforts.

Adjusted earnings were reported at $3.17 per share, this number looking quite clean, up about 7% on the year before. Net debt was reported at $1.85 billion and pegging EBITDA around $1.28 billion, leverage was very manageable, despite the continued past M&A efforts. Lower leverage was aided by the absence of M&A activity during 2023.

The company originally guided for 2024 (organic) sales to increase by 4-5%, with adjusted earnings seen up 7-9% to a midpoint of $3.42 per share. With shares largely trading around the $100 mark for most of 2024, share were not cheap at 29 times earnings, however, supported by a resilient balance sheet and great (long term) track record.

Delivering

Early in 2025, Church & Dwight announced its 2024 results in what otherwise has been quite a stable year, other than a transition of the CEO over the summer. Full-year sales rose by 4.1% to $6.11 billion, with adjusted earnings up 8.5% to $3.44 per share, both largely in line with the original guidance for the year. The composition of growth was sound, as an 4.6% increase in organic sales in itself was largely driven by a 3.3% increase in volumes.

Amidst the absence of acquisitions, net debt was down to $1.24 billion, with adjusted EBITDA, by my math, pegged at $1.40 billion, for a leverage ratio comfortably below 1 times, leaving ample of room for capital allocation.

Some of this money is allocated in the form of the dividend, which was hiked some 4%, now payable at $1.18 per share each year, for a yield around 1.2% at the $100 mark. This represents a payout ratio of around a third based on adjusted earnings power here, with more financial room for buybacks and/or M&A activity. This marks the 29th year in a row in which dividends have been hiked now.

Looking Ahead

For 2025, the company issued a downbeat guidance in the sense that revenue growth is seen at around 3%. This is actually driven by a 3-4% expected increase in volumes, with pricing and mix effects combined being slightly negative. Despite pricing being relatively soft in still somewhat of an inflationary environment, the company guided for a 7-8% increase in adjusted earnings per share, with earnings seen around $3.70 per share.

This means that shares trade at 27 times forward earnings, with real risks seen to this guidance, which is not cheap at all, although the company has seen leverage come down, of course. If the company delivers on this (volume) growth, investors likely continue to award shares a premium multiple, yet I fear, of course, quite a few risks to the guidance given the tariff discussion and impact on consumer spending (directly and indirectly).

With CHD shares trading largely flat in response to the tariff news, likely driven by a rotation of cyclical businesses to safer consumer staples, they have likely benefitted from a flight to safety. While Church & Dwight offers long-term security, it might be impacted a bit as well given these conditions, which in combination with the elevated valuations makes me cautious here.

data.title: GOOY: Alphabet-Linked ETF, A Prime Example Of YieldMax's Main Weakness
data.description:
YieldMax GOOGL Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:GOOY) is part of a unique set of ETFs that have caught investors' attention with their eye-popping yields. But that yield is only as good as the underlying stock's performance. And while we think Alphabet Inc. aka Google (GOOG), the underlying stock around which calls options are written and purchased and put options are sold to create the ETFs simulated covered call position, looks better to us long-term than most Magnificent 7 stocks, that's not saying a lot. They look awful as a group.

The main driver of GOOY (the GOOG stock price trend) is not firing currently, and might not for a while. Even when it does, it will likely coincide with a new bull market, and that will likely put me in the camp of owning the stock outright or collaring it, rather than deploying GOOY.

GOOG trades at a discount on the market and with some risks perceived (and possibly slightly exaggerated) by market participants. Its main business is advertising, more specifically through Google Search.

Rumors Of GOOG's Death Have Been Greatly Exaggerated

Is this a dying business? Well, the year 2024 showed us that it isn't, with search revenue showing double-digit growth. But the market believes the company's growth is uncertain in the long term, as more and more people (especially younger generations) are already using alternatives to search, Those include language learning models ("AI") or other social platforms like Instagram, Reddit (RDDT) and TikTok (BDNCE).

Additionally, the narrative suggests that Google isn't as far ahead in the AI race as Microsoft (MSFT), since ChatGPT still dominates in market share (with 60%) while Gemini has around 13%. For full disclosure, Gemini is my go-to for that type of research work (not here of course, since it is not permitted) and I find it to be quite effective. I have used it for some non-business projects, and it saves a ton of time, just as advertised.

GOOG's search revenue has shown no signs of slowing down in recent quarters. Its KPI (key performance indicators) are not only search volume but also advertising efficiency, monetizable fields, and other readily obtainable data points.

The table below shows diversified revenue. In addition to search, Google is exposed to secular trends such as Cloud, self-driving cars with Waymo, YouTube, and others.

GOOY Brings Less Volatility

This chart shows GOOY and GOOG since the inception of the former in 2023. It has been highly inadequate as a GOOG proxy, given that YieldMax typically aims for 80% tracking in both directions versus the underlying stock. A total return for GOOY over this time in the 15% range would be expected, but the gyrations in GOOG during this period have highlighted one of the structural weaknesses of GOOY.

And this next chart below shows that GOOY tracks well on the way up, but doesn't provide a cushion on the way down. That defeats the use case for it, based on this limited sample size.

Yield is nice, but not if you are essentially paying yourself with your own money. That's the situation here, effectively.

GOOG: Great Stock To Own, Eventually

Google's valuation is also very interesting, with a forward price-to-earnings below 17x, a very attractive level from several angles. Compared to its track record, it's a great multiple. Compared to other big techs or SPY, it's a very attractive multiple. Considering that Google is a company that will continue to grow its EPS close to double-digit over the next few years, it's also an excellent valuation.

That assumes, of course, that the market doesn't do a major downshift in its willingness to pay anywhere near 19x trailing earnings, or 31x trailing 5-year earnings, shown together here. This is the conundrum facing the whole stock market now. And it is way too early to determine if this is a flash-crash type of readjustment that is short-lived, or if this will morph into a secular shift.

That's why I'm a bit skeptical about that valuation grade below, but note how it has gone from F to D- in 3 months, as well as how growth has improved in some months and profitability has remained at A+. Momentum is also fair enough, since even with a falling knife, it's at C.

Below, these are unhappy technical charts in both daily and weekly time frames, in a broadly unhappy stock market. Still, I can stretch my imagination a bit and make a case that a bottom is getting closer because at least the stock has given up a year of gains.

GOOG also now sells at around its late 2021 price per share. That is the most bullish statement I can make about a nearly monopolistic operator in a growth space of the market, whose past has been so successful, that its original company name has become a verb ("google it").

But that's a lot of stretching in a market that is wholly unforgiving. And in turn, if I can't get up for owning GOOG just yet, GOOY is not a consideration. In addition, GOOY has not handled GOOG's limited price gains, which says more about the inherent weakness of that YieldMax style than anything else.

This article's bottom line is that GOOG is a watch list name I don't currently own, but that GOOY is not a way I'd expect to indirectly own GOOG with. Thus, GOOY gets a sell rating from us.

data.title: Hovnanian Enterprises: Cheap With Plenty Of Caveats
data.description:
Today, we put Hovnanian Enterprises, Inc. (NYSE:HOV) in the spotlight. Like most home builders, the stock has gotten hit hard in recent weeks. The shares are down just over 35% so far in 2025. The industry faces many headwinds right now. These include average 30-Year mortgage rates hovering near 7%, millions of existing homeowners with 'golden handcuffs' of having an existing mortgage under four percent, and near recent record low housing affordability. New tariffs on building materials like on Canadian lumber are just the most recent negative for the industry.

However, after the recent selloff, the stock of Hovnanian trades at a very low P/E and the equity is now under its book value per share. Time to buy the dip? An analysis follows below.

Hovnanian Enterprises is headquartered in New Jersey. The company is focused on building quality residential homes. Its communities range from entry-level homes, townhouses, and luxury builds. Hovnanian Enterprises also offers mortgage loans and title services. The stock currently trades around $85.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $520 million. Hovnanian's fiscal year begins on November 1st it should be noted.

Here is geographic breakdown of Hovnanian's land profile. It builds mostly wholly owned communities but also participates in some joint ventures.

Recent Results:

Hovnanian Enterprises posted its 1Q2025 numbers on February 24th. The company delivered GAAP earnings of $3.58 a share, more than 80 cents a share above the consensus estimates. Gross margin came in at 18.3%, within the range of previous guidance while Hovnanian delivered $72 million in adjusted EBITDA in the quarter, this was above previous management projections. It should be noted that gross margins fell significantly from the 21.8% level from the same period a year ago. This was even as management did a commendable job reducing SG&A expenses from 14.5% to 12.9% of revenues on a year-over-year basis.

Revenues rose 13.4% on a year-over-year basis to $673.6 million, missing expectations by just over $30 million. Hovnanian's non-home building revenues came in at $76 million of that total for the quarter and provided a solid contribution to net income.

New contracts rose from 1,279 in 1Q2024 to 1,400. Community count also rose on a year-over-year basis.

In addition, it should be noted that Hovnanian has been forced to offer an increasing number of incentives to move inventories. This primarily consists of mortgage rate buydowns. These incentives were 9.7% of the average sales price in the first quarter. This is up 160 basis points from the same quarter a year ago and a whopping 670 basis points above the levels of FY2022. Lumber prices are higher than a year ago and could go higher with the new tariffs. This is another potential negative for margins.

Management provided the following guidance for Q2. Gross margins are projected to be similar to Q1 while adjusted EBITDA is expected to come down.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Hovnanian Enterprises get little coverage from Wall Street. An investment firm called Zelman & Associates downgraded HOV to underperform from a neutral rating in mid-February. It has a $130 price target on the stock. Right after quarterly numbers hit the wires in late February, Wedbush maintained its Hold rating on the stock but lowered its price target all the way down to $120 from $155 a share previously. Those are the only current analyst ratings I can find on this home builder.

Just under 4% of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Insiders are not buying the recent dip in the stock quite yet. In late February through early March, several insiders sold just over $300,000 worth of equity collectively. That has been the only insider activity in the stock so far in 2025.

As far as the company's balance sheet, Hovnanian ended the quarter with $882 million of long-term debt and estimates its annual interest expense of just under $91 million. It should be noted its Debt Maturity schedule is in good shape.

Management has reduced its debt and leverage in recent years. That said, Hovnanian's balance sheet is not in as good of shape as Beazer Homes (BZH) which I profiled recently. It should be noted that Hovnanian does have $206 million of deferred tax assets. This means it will not have to pay federal income taxes on approximately $700 million of future pre-tax earnings, a true hidden asset.

Conclusion:

There is only one analyst firm out with projections for Hovnanian. They see $12.87 a share of profits in FY2025 on $2.95 million of revenues followed by $14.01 a share of earnings on $3.1 billion of sales in FY2026.

This leaves the stock trading around 6 times forward earnings and just under .2 times revenues. That is quite cheap on surface. However, home building is an extremely cyclical industry and almost always trades at a significant discount to the overall market. I need to see some signs that incentives and gross margins are stabilizing, and housing affordability is improving before I get more involved in this sector. I would also like to see an uptick in insider buying in these names as a sign of a potential bottom. Therefore, I am passing on any investment recommendation around HOV at this time.

One Final Caveat: There was a good article in the Wall Street Journal at the end of February and is pertinent to any investors in the home building sector. There were government programs initiated during Covid to keep FHA mortgage holders in their homes, even if they missed or did not make mortgage payments.

This overall program was extended several times, including on the last day in office of the previous administration. When that program ends in 2026 or if it is curtailed by the new administration, it will likely result in a flood of FHA foreclosures across the county. The current debt to income ratios of this subset is much worse than the housing crash of 2007-2010 (above) it should be noted. It is one of many reasons I have been underweight the housing sector for around 18 months now, and I will continue to be so until the underlying metrics improve.

data.title: Buy Certara After The FDA Announcement
data.description:
On Thursday, April 10, 2025, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration announced that it would phase out animal testing requirements for monoclonal antibody therapies and other drugs. Known as mAB for short, this type of therapy gradually replaced steroid-based treatments. In addition, mAB-based drugs have relatively fewer side effects. As a result, the FDA is advancing drug testing methods to embrace the latest technologies. This includes the use of artificial intelligence, data-driven modeling, and predictive simulation.

In reaction to the news, Certara (CERT) jumped by 15%. Will the new regulatory environment help the company increase its growth rate? Investors should look first at Certara’s fourth-quarter results.

Growth in the Fourth Quarter

In the last quarter, Certara reported non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.15. Revenue increased by 14.1% Y/Y to $100.4 million. For the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, however, it reported a loss of $12.05 million. This is down from a $55.36 million loss in FY 2023.

In Q4, the EBITDA adjustments included intangible asset amortization of $17.5 million and equity-based compensation of $7.7 million. The adjusted net income of $24.7 million deducted amortization of adjusted-related intangible assets at $14.4 million and equity-based compensation (also $7.73 million).

Chief Executive Officer William F. Feehery commented that the core biosimulation and Chemaxon businesses contributed to the company’s results. The CEO said that Certara would continue its investments in software in AI, which would strengthen its “end-to-end biosimulation offering that spans from drug discovery through the clinic.”

Chemaxon is a software solution that Certara acquired last year. The cheminformatics company provides “software solutions for property calculation and molecule design, chemical drawing, chemical search, and compound data management.”

AI As an Assistant

Certara did not hype AI by too much. It mentioned the term “AI” eight times on the conference call and (generative) “artificial intelligence” twice. Fortunately, the FDA announcement is a tailwind to Certara’s business strategy. The firm positioned the growth around capitalizing on the growing interest in biosimulation technology.

Investors may infer that the investments, which weighed on operating earnings, will pay off. The FDA news increases the attractiveness of holding CERT stock. In addition, readers may renew their interest in Schrödinger (SDGR). This firm sells scientific software to customers who develop computational tools in drug discovery and material science (according to the company’s ‘About us’ page).

Readers may also consider Ginkgo Bioworks (DNA) since they have an AI focus. However, Certara has a practical and cautious approach to how it spends on AI to enhance its product.

2025 Outlook

Before the FDA announcement, Certara issued a 2025 revenue forecast of between $415 million to $425 million. EBITDA margin will be in the range of 30-32%. At an adjusted EPS of $0.42 - $0.46, CERT stock trades at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of as low as 26.5 times.

Readers may compare Certara’s returns, Seeking Alpha Quant rating, and factor grades on this page. The default tickers include Schrödinger, Teladoc (TDOC), and Phreesia (PHR). In the table below, CERT stock ranks below that of its peers except for Evolent Health (EVH).

Investors should consider CERT stock more attractive than Teladoc. Shorts are betting heavily against the firm with a 15.3% short interest. The share price is “fading” to the downside because of the expensive acquisition of Livongo for $37 billion in 2020. Ever since the COVID lockdown ended and people chose to visit their doctor in person, Livongo’s costs weighed on its balance sheet.

Your Takeaway

The FDA is a tailwind for Certara. Previously reported GAAP losses should turn to break-even earnings as biotechnology firms adapt computational models. Compared to using animal models, this should give them faster results related to drug interactions and safety.

CERT stock is an attractive, speculative buy. However, wait for speculators to take profits in their position in the coming days. That would lower the share price and give investors a better entry price.

data.title: Built For The Tough Times: QHY's Edge In Today's High-Yield Market
data.description:
By Behnood Noei, CFA

Following last week's sharp sell-off in risk assets, investors have sought refuge in safer assets such as U.S. Treasury bonds. As a naturally more volatile segment of the fixed income market, high-yield (HY) bond spreads have widened by 92 basis points relative to Treasuries since April 2, 2025, "Liberation" Day." However, the sell-off hasn't been uniform across the HY spectrum. BB-rated bonds - considered the higher-quality tier within the HY space - saw spreads widen by 73 basis points, while the more speculative CC-rated bonds experienced a significantly steeper widening of 153 basis points.

Figure 1: Option-Adjusted Spread (OAS)

For many investors, fixed income plays a supporting role in their portfolios—not as a primary driver of returns, but as a source of diversification and stability during periods of market stress. That's why maintaining a quality bias within a fixed income allocation is so important. In this piece, we'll take a closer look at the WisdomTree U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Fund (QHY) and explore how its focus on higher-quality bonds, relative to both its peers and the broader Index, has contributed to its performance.

Higher Quality When It Matters

QHY has historically maintained a higher allocation to higher-quality bonds - and that's by design. Our investment approach, grounded in both fundamental and quantitative analysis, is intentionally structured to avoid lower-quality, potentially riskier issuers. As of the end of Q1, QHY held an over-weight position of approximately 9% in BB-rated bonds, while remaining under-weight in lower-rated segments such as B and below.

Figure 2: Ratings

This emphasis on higher-quality bonds has been reflected in QHY's performance. Year-to-date, and since April 2, 2025, the Fund has outperformed the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index by 62 and 10 basis points, respectively.

Figure 3: Relative Performance

A Timely Change

In our most recent rebalance (November 2024), we introduced equity return momentum as an additional factor to complement and enhance the long-term free cash flow signals in our model. This momentum metric evaluates a bond issuer's equity performance against other public issuers in the U.S. high-yield corporate debt universe. These momentum scores are then combined with the issuer's fundamental metrics to refine the bond selection process. The primary goal of this enhancement was to gain a timelier view of an issuer's financial health—and to further elevate the Fund's overall quality by identifying and excluding potential problem issuers before their fundamentals begin to weaken. A rough estimate suggests this change has contributed more than nine basis points in additional performance versus the previous methodology, underscoring the quality boost provided by incorporating momentum.

Figure 4: Momentum

Conclusion

In times of market stress, weaker issuers tend to get hit the hardest. That's where a steady focus on quality can make a real difference. QHY has shown how a thoughtful mix of fundamental research and timely quantitative insights—like the recent addition of equity momentum—can help it steer clear of the riskiest issuers. By tilting toward higher-quality bonds, QHY has delivered a better performance through recent volatility. For investors looking to stay invested in high yield without taking on unnecessary risk, a quality-focused approach like QHY may offer a more resilient way forward.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Fixed income investments are subject to interest rate risk; their value will normally decline as interest rates rise. High-yield or “junk” bonds have lower credit ratings and involve a greater risk to principal. Fixed income investments are also subject to credit risk, the risk that the issuer of a bond will fail to pay interest and principal in a timely manner or that negative perceptions of the issuer’s ability to make such payments will cause the price of that bond to decline. While the Fund attempts to limit credit and counterparty exposure, the value of an investment in the Fund may change quickly and without warning in response to issuer or counterparty defaults and changes in the credit ratings of the Fund’s portfolio investments. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

You cannot invest directly in an index.

Behnood Noei, CFA, Director, Fixed Income

Behnood Noei serves as Director of Fixed Income at WisdomTree Asset Management, where he develops the firm’s suite of fixed income and currency exchange-traded funds and enhances existing investment processes. Behnood has 11 years investment experience in portfolio management and quantitative research. Prior to joining WisdomTree in 2022, Behnood was a portfolio manager and developer of some of the fixed income ETFs at J.P.Morgan Asset Management, where he was directly responsible for managing more than 7 Fixed Income ETFs and multiple SMAs with more than $13Billion in assets. He graduated from The Ohio State University with Master of Science degree in Finance and is a CFA charter holder.

data.title: The Gold Standard: 5 Gold Mining Stocks To Dig Up Big Returns
data.description:
Trump’s Trade War & Falling US Dollar Unlocks a New Gold Rush

Grab your shovel. There’s a new kind of gold rush happening on Wall Street.

Maintaining a well-balanced commodities portfolio can be worth its weight in gold in terms of mitigating risk in volatile markets. Gold prices rose more than 1% on Friday, extending a multi-day rally as an escalating trade war between the U.S. and China, tariff fears, and a weakening U.S. dollar drove investors towards the safe-haven metal. ING Bank NV strategist Francesco Pesole wrote:

The question of a potential dollar confidence crisis has now been definitively answered – we are experiencing one in full force…The dollar collapse is working as a barometer of ‘sell America’ at the moment

Over the past year, gold has emerged as a favored safe-haven asset as investors seek to safeguard their portfolios from heightened volatility and rising trade tensions. In March, it exceeded $3,000 per troy ounce for the first time—its biggest price milestone since the COVID-19 pandemic.

Gold Prices Vault Past $3,000 (as of 4/11/25)

According to an analysis by the World Gold Council, a strengthening euro, economic uncertainty triggered by President Donald Trump’s back-and-forth tariff policies, and rising demand for Gold ETFs all propelled gold prices to record highs in March.

Tariff Fears and a Weakening Dollar Push Gold Price to New Highs

U.S. ETFs came in gold with $6B of net inflows, followed by Europe and Asia with 1B apiece. Meanwhile, a sweeping sell-off in equities fueled by tariff uncertainty further drove demand for gold in March.

Gold ETF Flows Surge in 2025

Last month, I recommended readers consider one of two Strong Buy Gold ETFs: the iShares® Gold Trust Micro ETF (IAUM) or SPDR® Gold MiniShares ETF (GLDM), depending on their preferred investment size. Both continue to eclipse the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY), which closely mirrors the S&P 500. Since recommending these funds on March 19, both have gained around 3.8% compared to SPY’s near 8.5% loss as of April 10.

SA Quant’s ETF Picks Crush the Broader Market

Looking ahead, softer-than-expected inflation data released on Thursday has prompted Fed futures traders to bet that the Federal Reserve will resume slashing rates in June, according to the CME FedWatch tool. Traders have also upped their expectations that the Fed would cut interest rates by 75 basis points in 2025 to a full percentage point by the end of the year. Slashed interest rates typically result in lower borrowing costs, which tend to benefit non-yielding assets such as gold.

82% of Fed Futures Traders Predict a June Rate Cut (as of 4/11/25)

Equities valuations remain high, forecasting further downside to equities should the economic slowdown continue. After the Trump administration’s sudden 90-day pause on most tariffs this week, the market exploded, but the euphoria quickly reversed again. While the tariff freeze may have prevented severe damage to the economy, concerns linger over the levies’ long-term effects. “We believe even these reduced tariffs will imply a serious hit to growth,” the UBS global research team warned on Thursday morning as the U.S. stock market retreated once again following Wednesday’s epic rally.

Amid this gloomy outlook for U.S. stocks, gold shines even brighter. “I think we’re very close, if not in, a recession now,” BlackRock (BLK) CEO Larry Fink told CNBC on Friday, adding that he did not think the U.S. was in a financial crisis and that “megatrends” in the economy like artificial intelligence would persist. J.P. Morgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon said Friday that he expects “estimates for corporate earnings to fall amid the uncertainty created by President Donald Trump’s trade negotiations.”

The comments from the leaders of the world’s largest financial institutions paint a fairly bleak picture for the economy in the near-term. Likewise, these comments can be viewed as positive for Gold’s continued rise. “We remain quite positive for gold,” Dominic Schnider, head of commodities and Asia Pacific currencies at UBS Global Wealth Management, told Bloomberg TV on Thursday. “The next step is going to be, at some point, the Fed coming in — and that gives the next leg up for gold.”

Major banks remain bullish that the price of gold will continue to rise: BofA’s head of metals research, Michael Widmer, predicts that the precious metal will rise to $3,500 in the long term, while Macquarie anticipates it will reach $3,500 by next quarter.

Investing in Gold Producers

Typically less correlated to traditional assets such as stocks and bonds, exposure to physical assets offers a myriad of benefits, from inflation protection to diversification to the potential for high returns.

As I've written before, gold ETFs can be a simple and effective way to gain broad exposure to the asset class. Alternatively, gold mining stocks can provide investors with exposure to the asset class, solid dividend income, and a layer of protection against market volatility. There are plenty of advantages to investing in gold producers. Unlike investing in the physical commodity, gold mining stocks often pay dividends, making them a compelling option for income-oriented investors seeking steady returns in volatile markets.

According to an analysis by DWS, gold mining stocks have grown more profitable in recent years after reducing their debt levels, increasing M&A, and placing a higher emphasis on cash generation. They even outperformed the price of gold for significant periods in 2024, a stark reversal of historical trends that shocked many industry insiders, as Reuters Breakingviews’ Edward Chancellor wrote.

Gold Mining Stocks Outperformed Gold on Several Occasions in 2024, Surprising Industry Insiders

Looking forward, Washington’s escalating trade war with China, lingering concerns over economic growth, and future interest rate cuts could potentially boost the performance of gold mining stocks even further. The SA Quant Team has five Strong Buy gold production stocks that have benefitted from gold’s extended rally, all of which possess strong balance sheets and excellent fundamentals.

We selected these stocks by reviewing the Top Gold Stocks quant screen. Our preferences were determined by focusing on Gold stocks that possess both solid growth metrics and an attractive valuation frameworks.

1. DRDGOLD Limited (DRD)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.3B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 1 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 1 out of 43

DRDGOLD is a South African mining group ranked first among all materials stocks and all gold stocks, producing gold from the retreatment of surface mine tailings. Headquartered in Johannesburg, the company commissioned one of the largest renewable energy initiatives in the industry by integrating solar photovoltaic (PV) technology and a renewable power grid into its operations to halve its usage of traditional energy sources.

DRD’s HY2025 results favorably reflected the rising gold price of gold, reporting a 28% increase in revenue and a 74% increase in operational profits to R1 578.7 million. Cash operating costs remained well-contained at R2 215.1 million. As SA analyst Alberto Abaterusso noted following its latest earnings results:

The outlook for DRDGOLD's profitability, a key driver of shares in the US stock market, is very promising as analysts continue to expect gold to be driven higher by strong demand for safe assets amid geopolitical risks and the Federal Reserve's rate cut. While gold is trading higher, the company is on track with higher throughput and lower costs.

DRD Stock Quant Breakdown

Surging gold prices are reflected by DRD’s ‘A+ momentum grade, with a three-month price performance of 56.2% compared to the materials sector median of -7.98%.

Its forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 13.2 is slightly cheaper than the sector at a -1.7% discount from the median, while its trailing price-to-earnings-growth (PEG) ratio of 0.48 similarly connotes a fair price tag. Profitability has improved from a ‘D’ to a ‘C’ over the past three months, boosted higher by return on total assets (TTM) of 16.6%, among other positive metrics.

In staying true to gold’s volatility-hedging qualities, DRD’s 1.4% dividend yield (TTM) offers steady, if modest, income with four years of consecutive payments. My next selection offers an even more attractive dividend yield.

2. AngloGold Ashanti plc (AU)

  • Market Capitalization: $19.6B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 15 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 7 out of 43

Formed in June 1998, AngloGold Ashanti (AU) is now one of the largest gold mining companies in the world, with operations in Africa, Australia, and the Americas. Last year, it completed its acquisition of Centamin, a gold mining and exploration company with mines in Egypt. This deal increased its annual gold production from 450,000 ounces to over 3M ounces, helping it surge more than 82% YTD. Its increased global footprint and the soaring price of gold helped it achieve record free cash flow last year.

Thanks to the Centamin transaction and strong tailwinds in the mining sector, AU has upped its production target this year to 2.90-3.23Moz, a 15% increase from 2024. As SA analyst Ivan Leyba recently wrote:

For this year, the company aims to surpass this milestone, which is why it has significantly increased its production target. This, coupled with the continued favorable macroeconomic environment for gold prices, could boost Anglo's operating and financial results to another level, helping to maintain the strong upward trend in its shares since the beginning of this year and putting it back on track to reach its highest levels of the last decade and a half.

AU Stock Quant Breakdown

AU’s biggest selling point is its 3.8% dividend yield, backed by an impressive five-year growth rate of 69%. It also boasts a strong dividend safety score, with a one-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 328%, an impressive 42,123% difference from the sector.

AU Dividend Safety Grade: ‘A-’

AU’s valuation has increased from a ‘D’ to a ‘C+’ over the past six months, with a forward P/E of 9.08, a 38% discount from the sector median. Growth is also strong, with forward revenue growth of 21.8%, a 665.5% difference from the 2.85% sector median.

Like AU, my next pick has drastically improved its production outlook, but its valuation remains cheap despite growing momentum.

3. SSR Mining Inc. (SSRM)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.06B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 4 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 2 out of 43

Headquartered in Denver, Colorado, SSR Mining (SSRM) produces gold, copper, silver, lead, and zinc across the United States, Canada, Turkey, and Argentina. Though its company's production guidance fell short of Wall Street expectations in Q4, shares are still up over 53% year-to-date as a result of the strong uptick in gold prices. It was recently upgraded by UBS after acquiring new Cripple Creek and Victor assets in Colorado, mitigating further downside from a heap leach failure at its Çöpler gold mine in Turkey.

A lapse in Turkish operations as a result of the Çöpler incident resulted in lower production in 2024. However, its powerful growth outlook and cheap valuation make it a compelling long-term investment. As SA analyst Taylor Dart observed:

SSR Mining is one of the better-run producers in the sector with disciplined capital allocation, a track record of per share growth, and several solid assets with diversification…However, the stock has been punished for being in an investment cycle with limited free cash flow generation this year and an outlook of lower production at higher costs. While this is certainly not the ideal setup…I think the negatives here are more than priced in and see this as a very attractive entry point for SSR Mining Inc. shares from a long-term investment standpoint while being paid to wait.

SSR Stock Quant Breakdown

Ranked second among all gold stocks, SSR’s Strong Buy status is upheld by excellent growth, strong momentum, and improving profitability at an attractive price. ‘A+’ momentum is reflected by three-month price performance of 34%, crushing the sector median by 9,984%.

Long-term EPS growth of 82.7%, a 680% difference from the sector median, underpins SSR’s recent acquisitions, increased production outlook, and the soaring price of gold amid intensifying demand. Profitability, which has improved from a ‘C+’ to a ‘B-’ over the past six months, is boosted by a levered free cash flow margin of 15%, an 189% difference from the sector. Finally, its forward PEG of 0.12, an 89% discount from the sector, reflects its strong growth outlook at an excellent price.

Similar to SSR, my next selection is a great choice for long-term, value-oriented investors looking for steady growth on the cheap.

4. IAMGOLD Corporation (IAG)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.9B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/10/25): 8 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/10/25): 4 out of 43

IAMGOLD Corporation (IAG), a Canadian miner headquartered in Toronto, produces gold across its home country and in Burkina Faso. The company has been leveling up production while bolstering its revenue growth since the opening of the Côté Gold mine, based in Ontario, last year.

After beginning commercial production in August 2024, the mine is expected to produce 250,000 to 280,000 ounces of gold in 2025, which IAG forecasts will result in a 33%-44% rise in production. That momentum has helped it surge more than 43% YTD as the rest of the market has struggled to stay in the green.

IAG February 2025 Presentation

IAG’s bullish growth outlook looks more attractive considering its low valuation, currently priced at just $6.88 per share. As SA analyst David Zanoni noted:

The stock's low valuation level leaves plenty of room to the upside for the stock. The combination of above-average revenue/earnings growth from a low valuation is a perfect recipe for the stock to outperform the broader market in 2025.

IAG Stock Quant Breakdown

IAG’s ‘A’ valuation grade is upheld by a top-tier forward P/E of 4.9, a 67% discount from the sector median. IAG’s low price tag comes with fantastic growth potential, with forward revenue growth of 38.2%, a 1,240% difference from the sector

Momentum is equally as powerful, with a six-month price performance of 43%, a 194% difference from the sector. With a net income margin (TTM) of 50% (a 1,003% difference from the sector) and return on total assets (TTM) of 15.25% (a 558% difference), IAG’s improving profitability is already shining through in the Quant metrics.

5. Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC)

  • Market Capitalization: $16.8B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/10/25): 14 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/10/25): 6 out of 43

Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) is a Canadian-based gold and silver miner operating six active gold mines across the U.S. (Nevada), Canada, Brazil, Chile, and Mauritania. Founded in 1993 and headquartered in Toronto, KGC’s Strong Buy rating is supported by its strong production, robust balance sheet, and high-quality assets in stable regions. The group reported strong 2024 results, achieving a record free cash flow of $1.34B, more than double the previous year.

The company reported an 8% quarterly decline in quarterly production, a drop-off stemming from dwindling supply at its Tasiast and Paracatu mines in Mauritania and Brazil. However, as KGC softens its production outlook for 2025, powerful industrial tailwinds more than make up for the difference. The Operating margins grew by 37% in Q4 compared to the 23% increase in the price of gold over the same period, taking full advantage of gold’s rapid price escalation.

KGC Stock Quant Breakdown

Despite its overall ‘D+’ valuation grade, KGC’s forward PEG of 14.2 comes at a near 5% discount to the sector. That could suggest that the miner’s shares are fairly priced when considering its long-term growth potential. Its forward P/E of 13.7 comes at a 1% premium to the sector. Growth appears strong across the board, with forward revenue growth of 10.5%, a 268.6% difference to the 7% sector median.

Momentum and profitability outshine the wider sector, with an ‘A+’ in both categories. KGC’s modest 0.92% dividend yield, while by no means the best in the industry, serves as an added bonus to its strong growth potential.

KGC, like the other four gold mining stocks discussed in this piece, make attractive options for investors looking to hedge their equity against volatility while gaining a layer of exposure to gold’s glistening rise.

Concluding Summary: A Golden Opportunity in Turbulent Markets

Gold has become a hot commodity for its inflation and volatility-hedging powers, and producers of the metals have profited immensely from its fantastic rise over the past year. The price of gold is likely to keep growing as the Fed looks to cut rates later this year and Trump’s 90-day pause on tariffs casts more uncertainty over the market.

The five Strong Buy mining stocks discussed in this piece can be a great way to diversify and mitigate downside risk as the broader market suffers in the face of economic uncertainty. Alternatively, if you’d like to gain broader exposure to the gold sector, consider using Seeking Alpha's ETF Ratings Screener tool to help find Strong Buy-rated gold ETFs.

data.title: AMD: Developing Continuously, But Still Not Enough
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) has not performed well year over year, falling from $200 in mid-2024 to our current $88. In this article, I will explain in detail the reasons for this decline, what AMD needs to do to reverse its decline, and why I am currently a "Hold" for AMD stock.

Why Did Stock Fell?

The primary catalyst for AMD's decline was its lackluster performance in the AI ​​chip market, which Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) continues to dominate. But AMD's failure to compete even in the gaming market, a market NVDA no longer cares about, has also compounded this effect. For example, despite AMD's efforts to gain momentum with its MI300-series accelerators in recent months, the company has struggled to present a compelling value proposition against NVDA's established ecosystem.

Performance in the data center sector is also below expectations. AMD's data center segment revenue, which serves as a proxy for AI chip revenue, increased by 69% YoY in Q4 2024, reaching $3.86 billion. Even though this is a very good development, the company fell short of expectations, as the market expectation was $4.14 billion. While its closest competitor NVDA is constantly breaking expectations, AMD's failure to meet expectations in the same sector is unfortunately seen negatively by investors and constantly exposes the stock to selling pressure.

CEO Lisa Su then said data center sales will shrink by about 7% in the first quarter of 2025, which adds doubts about AMD's near-term AI growth trajectory. Su is optimistic about tens of billions in AI chip sales over the next few years, but the market wants immediate proof of success from the company. Unfortunately, AMD has not yet been successful in providing that.

Late Entry to AI

AMD's relatively late entry into the AI ​​acceleration market has been costly for the company. AMD has entered the market with both technological leadership and ecosystem advantages that are difficult to overcome. AMD's $5 billion in AI-related revenue in 2024 pales in comparison to NVDA's $100 billion in revenue in the same period.

Beyond its long-standing rivalry with NVDA, AMD also faces new competition from companies such as Arm Holdings, which is expanding its influence from smartphones to cloud computing and networking equipment.

Ecosystem Dominance Race

AMD can't produce cards as powerful as NVDA. But even if it does, the ecosystem is in NVDA's hands. The biggest reason for this is CUDA software. CUDA software continues to be a critical barrier for AMD in the AI ​​market. To compete against this, AMD has invested in ROCm software and partnered with AI infrastructure providers like TensorWave, which only uses MI300X accelerators. While I really see this move to create an alternative as a positive, its adoption is just beginning. So it's not possible to see the results in the short term.

NVDA 70 Series G3D Mark Rating Performance Change

GTX 1070 13505 40.11%
