NVDA 80 Series G3D Mark Rating Performance Change
GTX 980 11117
GTX 1080 15575 40.10%
RTX 2080 18749 20.38%
RTX 3080 25147 34.12%
RTX 4080 34512 37.24%
RTX 5080 36133 4.70%
NVDA 90 Series G3D Mark Rating Performance Change
RTX 3090 26675
RTX 4090 38301 43.58%
RTX 5090 40096 4.69%

In the desktop graphics card segment produced for end users and gamers, I see AMD slowly catching up with NVDA. Especially since NVDA has almost no competitors from GTX 1000 to RTX 2000, AMD's attempts to take over the budget graphics card market were somewhat successful. The fact that NVDA's performance, especially in the RTX 5000 series, was almost no different from the 4000 series and that there were stock shortages in the global market were a fiasco. This situation directed users looking for budget graphics cards to more affordable AMD models. Currently, the gaming market is in favor of AMD, but since AMD still does not have software as good as NVDA, the potential of graphics cards is not being used sufficiently.

There is a really big opportunity for AMD to make a breakthrough in this period where NVDA is completely focused on AI and not giving as much importance to the gaming side (especially the low-budget card segment). I hope they see this as soon as possible and take a bit more aggressive stance.

There is one technology market that NVDA has not entered and that is desktop CPUs. I see AMD very well in desktop CPUs and I congratulate them. Especially before AMD released the Ryzen series before 2017, INTC was the leader in desktop CPUs and the biggest criticisms AMD received were about thermal issues. After AMD listened to this problem and solved it, they made better developments and caught up with INTC and gained some market share.

Considering the latest developments, INTC's extremely poor performance and problematic processors in the last 2 generations will be a lifeline for AMD, which now has the market share advantage in the desktop CPU market. I think AMD's relative competitiveness, especially in desktop CPUs, improved with each generation.

I can see the financial reflection of this development in the distribution of revenue by segment. AMD's client segment revenue, which includes desktop, laptop, etc. CPUs, grew by 58.32% YoY. That's why I can see that the company's market share gain against INTC is also reflected positively in revenue.

Roadmap of Innovation

The reason I've been so pessimistic so far is because I've been putting market movements into words. AMD's technological progress has been lagging behind its competitors and pushed the company down by as much as 58% since mid-2024.

The question I want to answer here is this. If the company can develop much more advanced and better competitive technologies in the coming periods, then $96 will be a cheap price for AMD. That's why I want to examine the company's roadmap and expectations.

CES 2025: A Showcase of AI and Gaming Innovation

At CES 2025, we had the opportunity to see AMD's innovations in AI and mobile computing. AMD introduced its Ryzen AI Max Series processors. As the name suggests, the processor is focused on AI and includes an XDNA 2 neural processing unit (NPU) that provides 50+ tera operations per second (TOPS) specifically for AI workloads.

These processors aim to deliver near-desktop performance to premium laptops while also providing seamless multitasking for AI-powered applications like chatbots and image rendering. In addition to that, Ryzen AI 300 and 200 series have been announced as more affordable, mainstream processors.

While NVDA doesn't have much of a competitive edge in AI, AMD's Instinct MI300 series accelerators have gained some momentum in AI infrastructure, with partners like Oracle and Vultr adopting their MI300X GPUs for cloud services. There's a slight problem here. Wolfe Research estimates data center GPU sales in 2025 to be $7 billion, well below estimates. The MI325X, which launched in the months leading up to 2025, and the MI350, which will be released in mid-2025, aim to close this gap. While the MI350 promises a 35x improvement in AI inference performance over its predecessor, it's a mystery whether this gap will ever close.

Financials

First of all, I'd like to talk about the distribution of AMD's revenue by segment. I have already mentioned that there is a 58.32% growth YoY in the client segment which accounts for 30.20% of the revenue. In addition to that, there is a 69.11% increase YoY in the data center revenue, which accounts for 50.39% of the company's revenue.

But there are clear declines in gaming and embedded segment revenues. Even though it does not constitute a large portion of the revenue, there is a 58.85% decrease in the gaming segment, which constitutes 7.35% of the revenue. This is an indication that despite gaining good market share, AMD has not made significant progress in the low-budget graphics card market. Finally, there is a 12.68% decrease in the embedded segment.

With the company's entry into the AI ​​sector, data center revenues have also increased at the same rate. This increase was also reflected in quarterly revenues. AMD's revenue, which was stagnant and stable at a certain level since 2022, started to increase QoQ again as of 2024. In its latest press release company stated that they expect $7.1 billion in revenue for the next quarter (there is a negative and positive $300 million deviation margin). So there is an expectation of a 24.25% - 35.21% increase in revenue YoY.

This expectation also means a decline from quarter to quarter, but this is due to seasonality. AMD CEO Lisa Su expects the data center segment to decline in line with the enterprise average while the consumer and gaming segments are expected to decline due to seasonal factors. She also emphasized that the decline in the segment in particular will slow down and stabilize.

I would like to analyze in more detail what the CEO means by seasonal decline, based on the company's qualitative developments. The concept of the "corporate average" is often used in financial analysis to provide a benchmark against which individual business segments can be compared. It represents a typical or standard measure of performance for the entire company, in this case, since the company has an expected 7% sequential decline in overall revenue, the decline in this segment will be in line with this.

Q1 2025 Q4 2024 Q1 2024 Q4 2023 Q1 2023 Q4 2022
Data Center $3,590.00 $3,859.00 $2,337.00 $2,282.00 $1,295.00 $1,655.00
Client $2,029.34 $2,313.00 $1,368.00 $1,461.00 $739.00 $903.00
Gaming $464.48 $563.00 $922.00 $1,368.00 $1,757.00 $1,644.00
Embedded $885.38 $923.00 $846.00 $1,057.00 $1,562.00 $1,397.00

Outside of data centers, there is an average of 12.26% and 4.08% revenue decline between the last and first quarters, respectively, due to seasonality in the client and embedded segments. I expect a decline of this magnitude again. It has been stated that the decline will slow down in the gaming segment compared to the last decline of 32.60%, so I expect a decline between 20-15% (average 17.5%).

As a result, my personal revenue forecast for the next quarter is $6.96 billion. That's about $140 million below AMD's own revenue forecast of $7.1 billion, but it's in line with the company's $300 million margin of error. I think this revenue is pretty healthy for the company going forward.

Economic Profit

Economic profitability, unlike accounting profit, is a calculation method that measures whether the stock has yielded more than investors expected based on the risks associated with the investment.

Unfortunately, AMD did not pass my test in this regard. In order to buy a company with peace of mind, I want to make sure that its economic profitability is above 0%. The value being above 0% means that the company is not only growing, but also growing in a profitable way. However, AMD's economic profitability, although slow since mid-2023, is still at -9.32%. So this is not a very positive situation.

Forward P/E Ratio

Since 2017, the company's forward P/E ratio has generally been higher than NASDAQ 100's (NDX) forward P/E. However, this has changed especially after 2024, and AMD is priced lower than the NASDAQ in terms of forward P/E. Considering that the company's historical average is 34.6x forward P/E, the company is currently in one of its cheapest positions in history. Although economic profitability does not present a positive outlook, the forward P/E shows that the stock is quite cheap compared to its history and the index it is in.

Charting

First of all, I want to talk about the company's price performance in the last 10 years. AMD stock closed 7 of the last 10 years with profit and 3 years with a loss. Its average return was 74.13%. That's why I can say that the stock's price performance in the last 10 years is not bad.

I will use various indicators and price action methods for detailed analysis. First of all, stock is really in a critical area. This $88 level is the area where the stock has found the most volume of purchases since 2021. So it is quite logical to expect the stock to hold around this area.

When I look at the RSI, I see that it is in the 31.92 (oversold) area. Even though moving averages are broken, and the stock seems to have entered a bear market, it is quite possible for it to hold at this level and rise to $120 (200 Week WMA).

But that doesn't mean I expect the stock to re-enter the bull market and reach its previous high. The stock needs to break at least the 200-week WMA and the price action resistance level of $120 and close above it for the week before I can expect another bull run. Unless that happens, I will continue to hold the stock because it could continue to oscillate between $120-88.

Risks

The biggest risk for AMD, as for any tech stock right now, is the Trump Tariffs. As it stands, semiconductors received a limited exemption from the first wave of tariffs, but that exemption was narrowly defined and excluded many chip-related products. The tariff landscape is particularly worrisome for AMD because the company generates most of its revenue from international markets, which makes it very exposed to trade policies.

AMD's advanced chips are also manufactured by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC). The fact that Taiwan is facing a 32% tariff further increases this risk. President Trump has made it clear that chip-specific tariffs are "coming soon" which implies that the current semiconductor exemptions are temporary. So I think a company like AMD which is all about chips can negatively be impacted by these tariffs in the short to medium term at least.

In addition to that, investors have increasingly turned away from big technology stocks following the U.S. presidential election by turning to sectors that are believed to benefit more directly from the new administration's policies, such as banking and cryptocurrency, which have negative effects. This rotation has increased selling pressure on semiconductor stocks such as AMD.

There is uncertainty surrounding AMD's operations in China, not just in the U.S. As U.S.-China relations become increasingly tense, potential restrictions on AMD's ability to sell AI chips in the Chinese market continue to be a constant concern for investors.

Conclusion

AMD's late entry into the AI ​​sector has created a significant disadvantage, but the company continues to compete with NVDA without interruption. Although it made positive developments by taking the CPU market from Intel (INTC) to a great extent, it is still lagging behind in the technology sector in general.

I think the company is in a very good position in terms of hardware, but it has shortcomings in terms of software. It needs to take the AI ​​and Data Center issue seriously and create its own ecosystem as soon as possible. In addition to that, since NVDA has reduced its weight in the affordable graphics card market on the gaming side, AMD should evaluate this opportunity and pay attention to the software of its graphics cards to be more aggressive in the competition.

In this process, while the economic profitability analysis gives a negative result, forward P/E ratio and charting shows that the stock may be cheap historically and compared to the sector. That's why in this complex environment I prefer to stay on the "hold" side.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Fairly Valued, But Recession-Resilient
data.description:
At the beginning of January 2025, I wrote my last article about Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and in the article I rated the stock as a “Buy”. I argued that Johnson & Johnson’s stock could serve as a high-yield bond alternative and in the conclusion, I wrote:

In about two weeks, on January 22, 2025, the company will report fourth quarter results for fiscal 2024. Not only are analysts rather bullish right now with no “Sell” and “Strong Sell” ratings in the last 90 days and only one “Hold” rating, but we could argue that the downside is limited, and the stock price might not drop much lower than $135. On the other hand, we should also not expect high-growth rates or the stock price going to the moon. Johnson & Johnson could be a solid investment, paying a decent dividend yield and rather serve as a high-yield bond alternative.

The call was right as the stock increased about 20% in value in the following weeks. But similar to almost every other stock in the world, Johnson & Johnson declined in value in the last few days. Nevertheless, the stock still increased about 4% (and 5% when including dividends) while the S&P 500 declined 8.5% in the same timeframe.

Before Johnson & Johnson will report first quarter results for fiscal 2025 on Tuesday, April 15, 2025, let’s look at the company and stock once again. Especially considering how volatile the stock market was in the last few days and what implications tariffs and a potential recession might have, an update about Johnson & Johnson seems necessary.

Quarterly Results and Full-Year Results

Before we look at the potential future, let’s look back at the last results Johnson & Johnson reported on January 22, 2025, for the fourth quarter and full-year fiscal 2024. In the fourth quarter, sales as well as earnings per share did slightly beat estimates and when looking at the results year-over-year, sales increased from $21,395 million in Q4 '23 to $22,520 million in Q4 '24 – resulting in 5.3% year-over-year growth. But while sales increased, earnings before provision for taxes on income declined by 19.5% year-over-year from $4,826 million in the same quarter last year to $3,887 million in this quarter. Earnings per diluted share declined from $1.70 in Q4 '23 to $1.41 in Q4 '24 – resulting in a 17.1% year-over-year decline.

While we see Johnson & Johnson struggling a little bit in the fourth quarter (especially with operating income and earnings per share declining), full-year results are better. Sales increased from $85,159 million in fiscal 2023 to $88,821 million in fiscal 2024 – resulting in 4.1% year-over-year growth. Earnings before provision for taxes on income increased 10.8% year-over-year from $15,062 million in fiscal 2023 to $16,687 million in fiscal 2024. And diluted earnings per share from continuing operations increased from $5.20 in fiscal 2023 to $5.79 in fiscal 2024 – resulting in 11.3% year-over-year growth.

Johnson & Johnson is reporting in two major segments – Innovative Medicine, which generated $56,964 million in revenue in fiscal 2024 (64% of total revenue) and MedTech, which generated $31,857 million in fiscal 2024 (36% of total revenue). And both segments reported growth rates in the mid-single digits in fiscal 2024. When looking at the sub-segments, especially Oncology and Cardiovascular are worth mentioning and reporting high-growth rates.

Oncology generated $20,781 million in revenue in fiscal 2024 and the sub-segment grew revenue 17.7% year-over-year. Cardiovascular generated $7,707 million in fiscal 2024 and the sub-segment grew its revenue 21.4% year-over-year.

Growth

Johnson & Johnson is certainly not a high-growth business, and investors should not expect high-growth rates from Johnson & Johnson. And the problem becomes obvious when looking at the different segments. While Johnson & Johnson is growing at a very high pace in its two sub-segments oncology and cardiovascular, but since other sub-segments are not growing, the top line isn’t increasingly rapidly.

And management is also optimistic for fiscal 2025, but adjusted operational sales will grow only between 2.0% and 3.0%. Operational sales are expected to be in a range of $90.9 billion and $91.7 billion. Adjusted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $10.50 to $10.70, resulting in 5.2% to 7.2% year-over-year growth.

For the first quarter of fiscal 2025, analysts are also not expecting Johnson & Johnson to grow at a high pace. Revenue is expected to be $21.59 billion, which would result in about 1% top-line growth compared to the same quarter last year. Earnings per share (on a GAAP basis) are expected to be $2.27 and, compared to $1.34 in the same quarter last year, it would actually result in a high-growth rate. But in my opinion, we should rather look at the adjusted earnings per share of $2.29 in the same quarter last year and in this case, the bottom-line would slightly decline.

Analysts have similar expectations for the years to come and assume earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 4.1% between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2033. And I don’t think we have much reason to be more optimistic. I already mentioned above that Johnson & Johnson can grow in the double-digits in some parts of its business – for example oncology and cardiovascular – but due to the large and very diversified business it is not enough when one or two sub-segments are growing with a high pace. It is also not enough for a business generating $90 billion in revenue to introduce two or three new blockbuster drugs. And Johnson & Johnson certainly has a promising pipeline.

Aside from growing by improving the top line, Johnson & Johnson – like every other business – can also grow by lowering expenses and improving efficiency. But I don’t think we should expect margin improvement to contribute in a significant way to bottom-line growth. I think it is possible for slightly higher margins to contribute about 1% growth annually, but we should not be too optimistic. Johnson & Johnson was trading for stable margins in the last few decades (which is actually a good sign for stability).

Additionally, Johnson & Johnson can use share buybacks to grow the bottom line and, in the past, the company has used share buybacks – but it was never a big part of its capital allocation strategy. During the last 20 years, the number of shares outstanding was reduced with a CAGR of 1.14%, and we can assume that share buybacks will contribute about 1% to bottom-line growth.

Overall, it is difficult to imagine that Johnson & Johnson will grow at a higher rate than in the low-to-mid single digits. In the last ten years, Johnson & Johnson grew its revenue with a CAGR of 1.80%. Operating income, however, increased only with a CAGR of 0.47% and earnings per share grew only 0.16% annually in the last ten years.

Recession

Looking at the growth rates and upside potential is certainly important, but we should also not ignore the flip side – the downside risk a business is facing. And not only due to the tariffs announced in the last few days we should always consider what impact a potential recession might have on a business.

When looking at the stock price performance of Johnson & Johnson in the last few decades, we see several drawdowns, but the steepest decline was in the 1970s when the stock fell 52.5% from its previous all-time high. All following corrections and bear markets took down Johnson & Johnson only about 40% or less and compared to many other stocks this is rather moderate.

Especially when looking at the S&P 500 in comparison, we see Johnson & Johnson performing much better in most cases. And performing better can have many different meanings: Usually it means that an investment in a certain stock is leading to a higher return and Johnson & Johnson slightly outperformed the S&P 500 over the last 30 years when including dividends.

But right now, the decisive question is not whether a stock can outperform the index during the bull run, the question right now is how we can limit the downside risk during bear markets and a potential recession. And Johnson & Johnson as a healthcare business might be able to perform well again during a downturn.

When looking at revenue as well as earnings per share in the last decades, we can’t claim that Johnson & Johnson was not affected at all by a recession, but it is performing solid. When excluding the years 2020 and the following, revenue hardly declined and earnings per share declined steeply on several occasions – but in almost no case we can make a connection between the declining earnings per share and a recession.

The argument for healthcare and pharmaceutical companies is very simple: The products Johnson & Johnson is selling are not only essential, but in some cases even essential for survival. Of course, surgeries can sometimes be postponed or in some cases it is possible to switch to cheaper alternatives for some pharmaceuticals. Nevertheless, a major part of Johnson & Johnson’s revenue can be seen as almost guaranteed.

I think we will see the fiscal guidance of countless companies being revised and analysts will also lower targets. In the case of Johnson & Johnson, we can be optimistic that the company will be able to meet its fiscal 2025 guidance.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

An important step in every analysis is to determine an intrinsic value for a stock to reach a decision if a stock is over- or undervalued. And for a first quick overview, I usually look at the price-free-cash-flow ratio and the price-earnings ratio. In the case of Johnson & Johnson, both valuation multiples indicate that Johnson & Johnson is not really expensive, but it is also not cheap or a bargain.

At the time of writing, Johnson & Johnson is trading for 25.6 times earnings, which seems rather expensive for a business growing only in the low-to-mid single digits, and it is also above the 5-year average of 23.10. When looking at the P/FCF ratio, Johnson & Johnson is trading for a valuation multiple of 19.95, which seems a little more acceptable and is also below the 5-year average of 22.63.

Especially with the recent crash, we can find several other stocks trading for similar valuation multiples – but these stocks are often growing at a much higher pace.

As always, we are not only looking at these simple valuation multiples but also use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock. Once again, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate as this is the minimum annual return we like to achieve. Additionally, we calculate with 2,429 million diluted shares outstanding and the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $18.06 billion.

Calculating with these assumptions, Johnson & Johnson has to grow its free cash flow slightly below 5% annually from now till perpetuity to be fairly valued. And considering the growth assumptions we made above, we can argue that these growth rates are reasonable, but I would also not assume that Johnson & Johnson is able to grow at a much higher pace.

However, I would argue that the free cash flow of the last four quarters might be a little bit too pessimistic as a basis. In the last four quarters, JNJ reported a free cash flow margin of 20.3% (meaning 20.3% of revenue ended up as free cash flow). The average of the last ten years, however, was 22.4%, and we can assume some margin improvement here. When assuming that 22.4% of fiscal 2025 revenue (according to guidance) will end up as free cash flow and assuming 5% growth from now till perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of $168.39, and we can see Johnson & Johnson as slightly undervalued at this point. However, it is also possible that 5% annual growth till perpetuity is a little too optimistic for Johnson & Johnson over the long run.

Conclusion

I would argue that Johnson & Johnson can be seen as fairly valued, and it is certainly not the perfect investment. But in a time of very high levels of uncertainty, limited stability and high volatility on the stock market, Johnson & Johnson might be a business worth considering.

I still am not expecting high-growth rates from Johnson & Johnson in the years to come, and the stock is certainly not a stock likely to double or triple in a few years. But I consider the stock as an investment that will provide some level of stability in volatile times and an investment with limited downside risk. Johnson & Johnson, as one of the major healthcare and pharmaceutical businesses might be more recession-resilient than many other businesses and over the next few years, this might actually be one of the most important criteria for any investment.
